coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots from surface-capping/passivation of small carbon nanoparticles with nanoscale titanium dioxide

Liang, Weixong
Yerra, Subhadra
Sulejmanovic, Dino
Nelson, Christopher
Lupini, Andrew

Singh, Buta
Sun, Ya-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are classically defined as small carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) with effective surface passivation, which has been accomplished predominantly by surface organic functionalization. In the current work, the passivation is achieved by the surface coating of CNPs with nanoscale TiO2 for CNP/TiO2 core/shell nanostructures, which are analogous to conventional semiconductor core/shell quantum dots (QDs). The TiO2 capping of CNPs results in substantial enhancements in the fluorescence quantum yields, also analogous to the similar enhancements famously known for the semiconductor QDs. Mechanistic implications of the findings, including the associated further validation on the classical definition of carbon dots, are discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carbon nanoparticles
Surface passivation
Core/shell nanostructure
Fluorescence enhancement

Y.-P.S. is grateful for the funding by NSF (2102021) and USDA (2023-67018-40681). The STEM experiments (C.T.N., A.R.L.) were conducted as part of a user project at the Center for Nanophase Materials Sciences (CNMS), which is a DOE Office of Science User Facility at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cartoon illustration on the structure of individual CDots (upper) and the CNP/TiO2 core/shell nanostructure.

Optical absorption spectra of the CNP/TiO2 in DMF dispersion (solid line) and aqueous dispersed CNPs (dashed line). Inset: photos of the “gel cake” before annealing (upper) and a DMF dispersion of the CNP/TiO2 (lower).
Powder X-ray diffraction results of the CNP/TiO2 sample. The calculated diffraction pattern is from the Cambridge database (Anatase TiO2, Cambridge database number 00–064–0863).
The TGA trace of the CNP/TiO2 sample over the temperature range 100–600 °C in the presence of air.
Fluorescence (FLSC) spectra of the CNP/TiO2 in DMF dispersion at 400 nm (F1) and 440 nm (F2) excitations are compared with that of the aqueous dispersed CNPs at 400 nm excitation (F3).

On the CNP/TiO2: (a) TEM image shows an agglomeration of nanoparticles. Simultaneously acquired (b) ABF-STEM and (c) HAADF-STEM images show atomic scale view of particles at the edge of a cluster. (d) Electron diffraction corresponding to the region in (a), and the circular boundary is the SAED aperture. Contrast is inverted and displayed with log scale. Outer circle is 15 1/nm. (e) Radial plot of mean diffraction intensity for the pattern in (d). Black lines correspond to d-spacings of anatase TiO2 weighted for XRD.

STEM - electron energy loss spectroscopy of CNP/TiO2. (a) HAADF image of the region measured by EELS. (b) Total EELS spectra with labeled characteristic core-loss peaks. (c-e) Elemental maps for Ti, O, and C.
The XRD results and estimates from scherrer equation.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots from surface-capping/passivation of small carbon nanoparticles with nanoscale titanium dioxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Clemson University, Clemson, SC 29634, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Clemson University, Clemson, South Carolina 29634, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Enrichment Science and Engineering Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, Tennessee 37830, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Enrichment Science and Engineering Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, Tennessee 37830, USA, and 3The Center for Nanophase Materials Sciences, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, Tennessee 37831, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Center for Nanophase Materials Sciences, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, TN 37831, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Center for Nanophase Materials Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Center for Nanophase Materials Sciences, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, TN 37831, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are classically defined as small carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) with effective surface passivation, which has been accomplished predominantly by surface organic functionalization. In the current work, the passivation is achieved by the surface coating of CNPs with nanoscale TiO2 for CNP/TiO2 core/shell nanostructures, which are analogous to conventional semiconductor core/shell quantum dots (QDs). The TiO2 capping of CNPs results in substantial enhancements in the fluorescence quantum yields, also analogous to the similar enhancements famously known for the semiconductor QDs. Mechanistic implications of the findings, including the associated further validation on the classical definition of carbon dots, are discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDots) [1–21] are classically defined as small carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) with effective surface passivation [1,3,21]. The CNPs (such as those of average sizes on the order of 5 nm in diameter) could be harvested from soot samples produced by the laser ablation of carbon targets, the arc discharge of graphite rods, or commercially supplied carbon nanopowder samples. Since the discovery of CDots, the effective surface passivation of CNPs has been achieved successfully by chemical functionalization with selected organic species, such as the use of oligomeric polyethylene glycol diamines (PEG1500N for average molecular weight ∼ 1500) and oligomeric polyethylenimines (PEI) in the chemical functionalization of CNPs for PEG1500N-CDots and PEI-CDots, respectively [22,23]. Structurally CDots may be considered of a special core/shell configuration (Fig. 1 ), each with a CNP core and a corona-like soft shell filled with organic species of the chemical functionalization, thus at least phenomenologically similar to the core/shell nanostructure commonly found in conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [23–26].

For the CdSe/ZnS core/shell nanoparticles among the most famous semiconductor QDs as an example, the wider bandgap ZnS shell is generally considered serving the function of protecting the photoexcited CdSe core from wasteful deactivation, or more specifically stabilizing the surface traps on the core CdSe nanoparticle to enable more effective and efficient formation of the emissive excited states [24–26]. Among experimental evidence for such a consideration is the observed significant enhancement in the fluorescence emission intensities (quantum yields) of the CdSe nanoparticles upon ZnS capping. One may argue that the same consideration could be applied to the observed orders-of-magnitude increases in fluorescence emission quantum yields from “naked” CNPs in various solvent suspensions [27] to CDots in which the abundant surface defects of CNPs must be effectively passivated by the organic functionalization [3,23,28–30]. Interestingly, despite the fact that carbon is generally not a member of the semiconductor family, CNPs are exhibiting nanoscale semiconductor-like properties in the photoexcited states, with similarly rapid charge transfers following photoexcitation and the charge separation for the separated electrons and holes, and also with the surface defect sites trapping some of the separated redox pairs and thus interrupting their radiative recombinations [3]. Further supporting the striking similarity was the recent finding that the capping of CNPs with nanoscale ZnS in the same core/shell configuration as that found in conventional semiconductor QDs could provide sufficiently effective surface passivation of CNPs for the CNP/ZnS nanostructure to exhibit similarly bright and colorful fluorescence emissions found in classical CDots [31].

In this study, sol-gel processing was employed for the surface coating of CNPs with organotitanium, followed by the hydrothermal annealing for CNP/TiO2 core/shell nanostructures. Upon the characterization of the CNP/TiO2 sample by using a series of instrumental techniques, the sample in solvent dispersion was found to be considerably more fluorescent than the similarly dispersed naked CNPs, with the observed fluorescence quantum yields comparable to those of representative CDots from the classical synthesis. The significance of the results to the understanding of CDots versus conventional semiconductor QDs and related mechanistic and technological implications are discussed.

Carbon nanopowder sample was obtained from US Research Nanomaterials, Inc. Titanium(IV) ethoxide (99 %) was purchased from Beantown Chemical, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8 %) and hexanes (HPLC grade) from Fischer Scientific, and nitric acid (HNO3, 68–70 %, ACS grade) from VWR.

UV/vis absorption was measured on a Shimadzu UV-1280 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were acquired on a Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorometer equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp. Fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) were determined by using the relative method with 9,10-bis(phenylethynyl)-anthracene (BPEA) in cyclohexane as the standard. X-ray powder diffraction was performed on a Rigaku SmartLab instrument equipped with a sealed Cu source, zero-background sample holder, and a HyPix-3000 2-D detector. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a TA Instruments SDT600 instrument with the heating profile of 10 °C/min from 25 °C to 600 °C. Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) were performed on a Nion UltraSTEM U100 at 100 kV with a cold field emission gun and an Dectris ELA detector. TEM and scanning area electron diffraction (SAED) were collected at 300 kV on Thermo-Fisher Titan STEM equipped with a X-FEG high brightness Schottky field emission gun and a Fischione HAADF STEM detector. The sample specimens of CNP/TiO2 for TEM and STEM were prepared from a 0.172 mg/mL in DMF source solution, sonicated for 10 min, and drop cast on a 300 Ni mesh lacey carbon TEM grid. The TEM and diffraction characterization on the Titan were performed without further processing of the sample. The STEM imaging and STEM-EELS on the Nion were performed after the sample underwent a vacuum bake at 80 °C for 20 h and storage under vacuum conditions for an additional 5 days.

The carbon nanopowder sample (4 g) was refluxed in HNO3 (8 M) for 48 h. Upon cooling, the sample mixture was repeatedly washed with clean deionized water (300 mL), coupled with rota-vap, until pH ∼1, followed by concentrating to about 50 mL and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to collect the supernatant for dialysis. The dialysis was in a membrane tubing (MWCO ∼500) against fresh water, which was changed every 2 h for a total of 48 h. Collected from the dialysis tubing was an aqueous suspension of CNPs for uses in further experiments.

For the sol-gel processing to cap CNPs with nanoscale TiO2, the sol was prepared by mixing Ti(OEt)4 (2.9 mL) into ethanol (51 mL), and to the mixture was added HNO3 (16.5 M, 0.16 mL) and water (0.43 mL), followed by refluxing at 80 °C for 1 h. To the sol in refluxing was added a concentrated aqueous dispersion of CNPs (∼80 mg/mL, ∼2.5 mL). The sol was vigorously stirred to prevent gel formation and ensure homogeneous mixing. After 30 min, the sol with CNPs was briefly filtered to obtain a homogenous gel-like brown “cake” of CNPs dispersed in Ti(OH)4 matrix (a good mixture of CNPs with Ti(OH)4). Upon the slow evaporation of ethanol from the gel overnight, the resulting black colored sample was annealed in the presence of water (5 mL) in a sealed stainless steel vessel acquired commercially, with the temperature profile of 10 °C/min heating to 350 °C, at 350 °C for 2 h, and then 10 °C/min cooling back to room temperature. The annealed sample retrieved from the vessel was filtered, washed with water and dried in a vacuum oven, and then grounded to obtain a sample of brown colored fine powders. The sample was added to DMF, and the resulting suspension was vigorously sonicated for 3 h, followed by centrifuging at 20,000 g for 5 min to yield the colored dispersion of nanoscale TiO2-coated CNPs (CNP/TiO2) in DMF.

In the surface capping of individual CNPs with nanoscale TiO2 for CNP/TiO2 core/shell nanostructures, the sol–gel approach was employed. The organotitanium compound Ti(OEt)4 was used to make the sol, in which the pre–processed and dispersed CNPs were immersed for a sample looked like a “gel cake” (Fig. 2 ). The homogeneous appearance of the sample was consistent with the targeted sample structure in which individual CNPs were each surrounded (equivalent to capped) by many Ti(OEt)4 molecules, thus locally the CNP/[Ti(OEt)4]n core/shell configuration ready for dehydration to convert [Ti(OEt)4]n to nanoscale TiO2. The hydrothermal annealing of the sample in a sealed vessel was effective for the dehydration of [Ti(OEt)4]n, with the advantage of preserving some of the dispersion characteristics of the sample post-annealing. Among the solvents tested for the dispersion of CNP/TiO2 with vigorous sonication, water and ethanol were not effective, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) significantly better, and dimethylformamide (DMF) the best. The DMF dispersion of CNP/TiO2 was stable with an appearance no different that of a typical colored solution (Fig. 2).

The trace of TGA temperature scan for the CNP/TiO2 sample in the presence of air (Fig. 4 ) suggests the initial loss of about 2 % over the temperature range up to 150 °C due probably to the residual water trapped in the dot structure, and then the loss of about 14 % before reaching the plateau around 500 °C that should be attributed to the carbon in CNPs being oxidized into CO2 gas. The 84 % residue must be TiO2, thus the TiO2 to carbon mass ratio of 84/14 = 6. With the average size of CNPs around 5 nm in diameter, the estimated average thickness of the TiO2 shell in the CNP/TiO2 core/shell configuration is about 1.4 nm under the assumption that the shell is uniform. In reality, however, the TiO2 shell thickness may be very uneven, probably with some areas of the CNP surface coated with much more TiO2 that would appear to XRD (Fig. 3) as TiO2 nanoparticle-like features of around 5 nm in average diameter.

The “naked” CNPs, which are CNPs with some surface-bound carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing groups due to the oxidative acid treatment in their processing, in aqueous or other solvent suspensions are not, or are very weakly fluorescent, with observed quantum yields generally less or much less than 0.5 % [3,27]. Their effective surface passivation via organic functionalization in CDots, which is conceptually and likely also mechanistically similar to the surface capping of conventional semiconductor nanoparticles that reduces the deactivation due to surface defects and therefore enhances the radiative recombination for the emissive excited states, results in substantially higher fluorescence quantum yields, typically 10–15 % for PEI–CDots as a representative example, accompanied by significant narrowing of the fluorescence emission bands [23,36]. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CNPs immersed in the Ti(OEt)4 gel cake is very broad, largely the same as those of CNPs in various solvent suspensions [27], and the spectra of the CNP/TiO2 dispersed in DMF are significantly narrower in the band width, which is typical of CDots, with also the characteristic excitation wavelength dependence [37]. The observed fluorescence quantum yields of the CNP/TiO2 sample, 12 % at 400 nm excitation and 14 % at 440 nm excitation, are also comparable to those of PEI-CDots [23,36].

For an atomic scale real space view, the CNP/TiO2 sample was characterized using aberration corrected TEM and STEM. The CNP/TiO2 nanostructures on the TEM grid are principally in agglomerated clusters such as that shown in Fig. 6 . Atomic scale images at the cluster edge in Fig. 6-b&c depict the crystalline nanoparticles of ∼5 nm in size. These images were taken simultaneously from annular bright field (ABF) and high angle annular dark field (HAADF) detectors. The ABF image (Fig. 6-b) is relatively more sensitive to light elements such as carbon. The HAADF image (Fig. 6-c) intensity scales with sample thickness and atomic number (Z) by ∼Z1.8, so Ti (Z = 22), O (Z = 8), and C (Z = 6) appear bright compared to the vacuum with single atom contrast ratios of ∼13.4, 1.8, and 1, respectively. Thus, the HAADF image includes compositional information with the signal heavily weighted towards heavier elements, highlighting Ti in this case. The various TiO2 sizes and shapes in the sample are consistent with the earlier discussion on the XRD and TGA results suggesting that the TiO2 coatings must be very uneven. The electron diffraction of the sample is shown in Fig. 6-d&e, corresponding to the 160 μm diameter region circumscribing Fig. 6-a, which is the selected area electron diffraction aperture used. The SAED pattern (Fig. 6-d) exhibits diffraction rings consistent with a polycrystalline/nanoparticle sample. Expressed as a radial diffraction intensity the diffraction peaks match very well with the labeled anatase TiO2 (Fig. 6-e) in agreement with XRD (Table 1).

The compositional information provided by the HAADF-STEM did not clearly elucidate the CNPs versus TiO2 morphology, so we next turned to electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS), with the results shown in Fig. 7 . As preparation for the ultrahigh vacuum (UHV) environment of this experiment, the samples were subjected to a 80 °C 20 h vacuum bake out, a lower temperature than the standard protocol with the intent to avoid overly impacting the native CNP distribution/morphology while still reducing contamination/outgassing of the sample. A region characterized by STEM-EELS is shown in atomic resolution HAADF in Fig. 7-a, which consists of several nanoparticles on the edge of the lacey carbon support. The summed EELS spectra collected over this region, as shown in Fig. 7-b, have clear characteristic signatures of the Ti, O, and C elements. Individual elemental maps in Fig. 7-c&d show their distributions, indicating that Ti and O very closely correlate with each other and with the regions of higher HAADF intensity, which contain anatase TiO2 nanoparticles. Carbon, however, is principally observed in the lacey carbon support and collected on the exterior of the particle cluster. The latter is likely carbon contamination given its accumulation on the sample surface, and this is an expected, even unavoidable, form of contamination even in UHV systems as utilized here, especially given the reduced sample bake-out temperature. As a result, it leaves some uncertainty of the native CNP distribution. At least for the particles observed here, the CNPs do not noticeably create negative space in the interior volume of the TiO2 particles nor are they positively detected above the carbon contamination level. The use of EELS served as an elemental analysis but also indicated the building up of carbon on the surface of the TiO2 during the data collection, further complicating the visualization of CNP/TiO2 interactions. Each of the images reveals the labeled elements of the nanoparticles on the lacey carbon holder.

Collectively the electron microscopy results confirm the substantial presence of TiO2 and its coexistence with carbon, although the effort to visualize the CNP/TiO2 interactions was complicated by contamination. Nevertheless, the results are consistent with the expected coating of CNPs by nanoscale TiO2. The nanoscale structure in the sample must be dominated by the CNP/TiO2 core/shell configuration or effectively the equivalent such that at the length scale on the order of 10 nm the mixing of CNPs with TiO2 is effectively the same as the former being capped by the latter. Further supporting such an assessment is the optical spectroscopic response to the apparently effective surface passivation of the CNPs, as reflected by the orders-of-magnitude enhancement in observed fluorescence quantum yields from those of naked CNPs.

The surface capping of CNPs by TiO2 in the core/shell nanostructure and the associated enhancement effects on the photoexcited state properties and processes are rather similar to those of the ZnS capping for CNP/ZnS reported previously [31]. For the CNP/ZnS, borrowed from the established protocol for CdSe/ZnS QDs were some of the key experimental procedures and chemical reagents including surfactants and other organic molecules [25,26]. The CNP/ZnS core/shell nanostructures with some organic species on the surface are more advantageous in terms of their being mostly individually dispersed in selected solvents, though the involvement of surfactants and other organic species could also invite questions on the possible contribution to the surface passivation of CNPs by the organic matters. The CNP/TiO2 core/shell nanostructures in this study are apparently more aggregated even in solvent dispersions due to the absence of organic species in the sample, but on the other hand represent an unambiguous example for the effective surface passivation of CNPs by the TiO2 capping only. The fact that CNPs could be effectively surface passivated by not only organic functionalization but also the capping of inorganic salts, including also the organic and inorganic combinations [38], to materialize the properties characteristic of CDots supports strongly the classical definition of CDots as effectively surface passivated CNPs.

In summary and conclusion, we show that CNPs can be capped by nanoscale TiO2 for CNP/TiO2 core/shell nanostructures of considerably improved photoexcited state properties from those of naked CNPs. This improvement is comparable to that seen with the similar surface capping of CNPs by nanoscale ZnS, despite the fact that TiO2, unlike ZnS, is hardly considered a wide bandgap semiconductor. The capping effects in the core/shell nanostructures must be the effective surface passivation of CNPs resulting in much lower loss of the photoexcitation energy to the surface defect traps on CNPs, which is not only phenomenologically but also to a significant extent mechanistically analogous to the passivation effects achieved in the conventional semiconductor QDs. More experimental exploration and mechanistic elucidation of the surface capping or more broadly the effective surface passivation of CNPs for CDots, in reference to and comparison with the similar effects in semiconductor core/shell QDs, will prove interesting and valuable.

Kirkland Sheriff: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Weixong Liang: Investigation, Data curation. Subhadra Yerra: Investigation, Data curation. Dino Sulejmanovic: Investigation. Christopher Nelson: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Andrew Lupini: Investigation, Data curation. Shiou-Jyh Hwu: Supervision. Buta Singh: Investigation. Ya-Ping Sun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: K.S. acknowledges the support of the DOE Office of Science Graduate Student Research (SCGSR) program administered by the Oak Ridge Institute for Science and Education for DOE under contract number DE–SC0014664. Y.-P.S. is grateful for the funding by NSF (2102021) and USDA (2023-67018-40681). The STEM experiments (C.T.N., A.R.L.) were conducted as part of a user project at the Center for Nanophase Materials Sciences (CNMS), which is a DOE Office of Science User Facility at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
